critique.summary: Google engineers have developed and open-sourced Sashiko, an agentic AI system designed to review Linux kernel code. The system reportedly detects about 53% of bugs in recent kernel issues, surpassing human reviewers in some cases, and is funded by Google with hosting moving to the Linux Foundation.
mainPoints: Sashiko is an AI code review tool developed by Google engineers for the Linux kernel.
It has been used internally at Google and is now publicly available as open-source software.
Sashiko reportedly detects 53% of bugs in a sample of 1,000 recent Linux kernel issues tagged with 'Fixes:'.
The project is funded by Google and is transitioning hosting to the Linux Foundation.
Sashiko was designed to work with Google Gemini Pro 3.1 but is compatible with other large language models like Claude.
A web interface for Sashiko is available at Sashiko.dev.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Sashiko's development by Google engineers and its open-source availability are supported by the article and corroborated by the presence of the project on GitHub and the Linux Foundation involvement. The reported 53% bug detection rate comes from an internal measurement by Roman Gushchin, a credible source as a member of Google's Linux kernel team. However, independent verification of the bug detection rate and comparison to human reviewers is not provided, so the effectiveness claim should be viewed cautiously. The article does not cite multiple independent sources or provide alternative perspectives, limiting the ability to fully verify all claims.
considerations: The article does not provide independent third-party verification of Sashiko's bug detection performance.
Potential limitations or false positive rates of Sashiko are not discussed.
The impact of Sashiko on the Linux kernel development workflow and human reviewers is not explored.
Details about the underlying AI models and their training data are not provided.
The article does not discuss privacy or security implications of using AI for kernel code review.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Google's Sashiko AI tool for Linux kernel code review, with commenters debating its effectiveness, usability, and potential impact on development workflows. Key concerns include the tool's bug detection accuracy, false positive rates, user interface clarity, and the implications of automated style changes.

comments.author: kleiba
comments.text:
> Sashiko was able to find around 53% of bugs

That's cool. Another interesting metric, however, would be the false positive ratio: like, I could just build a bogus system that simply marks everything as a bug and then claim "my system found 100% of all bugs!"

In practice, not just the recall of a bug finding system is important but also its precision: if human reviewers get spammed with piles of alleged bug reports by something like Sashiko, most of which turn out not to be bugs at all, that noise binds resources and could undermine trust in the usefulness of the system.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly highlights the importance of precision alongside recall in bug detection systems, emphasizing that high false positives can undermine trust and waste resources.

comments.author: rwmj
comments.text:
Better to link to the site itself, or one of the reviews?

For an example of a review (picked pretty much at random) see: https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260318151256.2590375-1-andr...

The original patch series corresponding to that is: https://lkml.org/lkml/2026/3/18/1600

Edit: Here's a simpler and better example of a review: https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260318110848.2779003-1-liju...

I'm very glad they're not spamming the mailing list.
comments.analysis: Provides useful resources and examples to better understand Sashiko's output, contributing constructively to the discussion without speculation.

comments.author: withinrafael
comments.text:
Looks cool, but this site is a bit difficult for me to grok.

I think the table might be slightly inside-out? The Status column appears to show internal pipeline states ("Pending", "In Review") that really only matter to the system, while Findings are buried in the column on the far right. For example, one reviewed patchset with a critical and a high finding is just causally hanging out below the fold. I couldn't immediately find a way to filter or search for severe findings.

It might help to separate unreviewed patches from reviewed ones, and somehow wire the findings into the visual hierarchy better. Or perhaps I'm just off base and this is targeting a very specific Linux kernel community workflow/mindset.

Just my 1c.
comments.analysis: Offers a user experience critique of the Sashiko interface, suggesting improvements to make bug findings more accessible and understandable.

comments.author: monksy
comments.text:
I think this is a great and interesting project. However, I hope that they're not doing this to submit patches to the kernel. It would be much better to layer in additional tests to exploit bugs and defects for verification of existance/fixes.

(Also tests can be focused per defect.. which prevents overload)

From some of the changes I'm seeing: This looks like it's doing style and structure changes, which for a codebase this size is going to add drag to existing development. (I'm supportive of cleanups.. but done on an automated basis is a bad idea)

I.e. https://sashiko.dev/#/message/20260318170604.10254-1-erdemhu...
comments.analysis: Raises valid concerns about automated patch submissions and advocates for verification through testing, highlighting potential risks of automated style changes in large codebases.

comments.author: mika-el
comments.text: the separation between who writes and who reviews is the whole thing. I do same at smaller scale — one model writes code, different model reviews it. self-review misses things, same reason you don't review your own PRs
comments.analysis: Provides insight into the value of separating code generation and review processes to avoid oversight, aligning with established software development practices.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text:
Now they want to kill the Linux kernel. :(

We've already seen how bug bounty projects were closed by AI spam; I think it was curl? Or some other project I don't remember right now.

I think AI tools should be required, by law, to verify that what they report is actually a true bug rather than some hypothetical, hallucinated context-dependent not-quite-a-real-bug bug.
comments.analysis: Expresses concern about AI-generated spam in bug reports affecting open source projects, a known issue, though the tone is somewhat alarmist.

comments.author: ChrisArchitect
comments.analysis: Simply shares the official GitHub repository link, providing a useful resource but adding little analytical content.

comments.author: quantium1628
comments.text: b2b or b2c? feels like it could go either way
comments.analysis: This comment is vague and does not contribute meaningful insight to the discussion about Sashiko.

comments.author: 4fterd4rk
comments.analysis: An emotional and dismissive comment lacking constructive content or explanation.

comments.author: qainsights
comments.text: They would have completely redesigned Google Gerrit.
comments.analysis: Makes an unsubstantiated claim without evidence or context, adding little value to the discussion.
